summary: Gruber addresses objections that the iPhone's lack of Flash contradicts Apple's claim that the iPhone offers 'the real Internet.' He argues that Apple's 'Watered Down' commercial makes a general advertising claim, not a specific technical promise, and that the phrase 'just the Internet' doesn't imply support for every web plugin. He notes that if the standard were total feature parity with desktop Safari, then Java and Windows Media would also be missing. He suggests Apple may be strategically framing HTML, CSS, and JavaScript as the 'real' web, positioning Flash as non-standard despite its prevalence.
key_points: Apple's 'just the Internet' iPhone claim is advertising rhetoric, not a specific technical promise of feature parity with desktop browsers
If Flash's absence contradicts the 'real Internet' claim, then so does the absence of Java and Windows Media — the argument proves too much
Apple may be deliberately framing HTML/CSS/JavaScript as the only technologies that constitute the 'real' web, marginalizing Flash as non-standard
Gruber remains open to the possibility that Flash support could arrive with the upcoming SDK release, but doubts it
money_quotes: "It's just the Internet" is not a specific technical claim. For god's sake they're using "Internet" to mean "web".
If you think "just the Internet" implies that MobileSafari should support everything Safari for Mac and Windows supports, that means more than just Flash is missing, including Java and Windows Media.

It's also possible that this sort of phrasing is an attempt to frame the issue in Apple's favor, by establishing HTML, CSS, and JavaScript as the only technologies that constitute the "regular" web — and imply that Flash, no matter how common, is non-standard.
key_insight: Apple's 'just the Internet' claim is advertising shorthand that strategically equates the web with open standards like HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — implicitly recasting Flash as a non-standard add-on rather than an essential part of the web experience.
